what_manipulated: perceived financial scarcity; exposure to interventions reducing scarcity perception
what_measured: loan decision quality; subsequent reborrowing; cost calculation accuracy; decision-making process changes
experiment: Participants in an experimental condition experienced a financial scarcity prime while making loan decisions; qualitative data from a government agency were compared to validate findings. Two intervention conditions tested whether reducing scarcity perceptions improved subsequent decision quality. The primary comparison is between the scarcity-primed and control conditions, with secondary triangulation across experimental and qualitative data sources to assess the consistency of the scarcity effect on financial decision-making.

what_manipulated: monetary scarcity exposure; contextual variation in everyday situations
what_measured: frequency and automaticity of cost-related thoughts; mental associations involving economics; attentional capture and suppression difficulty
experiment: Units with lower income and units with higher income were compared on the frequency, automaticity, and interference of cost-related cognitions in daily life. The study examines naturally occurring variation in monetary scarcity without active experimental manipulation, measuring the salience and persistence of economic thoughts through self-report. Cognitive patterns are assessed as correlates of financial circumstances.

what_manipulated: stimulus type (conditioned fear-associated vs. neutral vs. extinguished fear-associated); recall test timing (next day after training)
what_measured: oscillatory neural activity in anterior midcingulate cortex and ventromedial prefrontal cortex; skin conductance response; differential theta and gamma power
experiment: Units underwent a differential conditioning protocol followed by extinction training, with a recall test on the next day. During the recall test, task-irrelevant fear cues and neutral cues were presented while EEG and skin conductance were recorded. Comparison of oscillatory activity and physiological responses to conditioned versus extinguished stimuli identifies regional signatures of fear expression and extinction recall. Individuals with intact extinction recall are compared to poor suppressors on neural markers.

what_manipulated: social context (solo exercise vs. paired exercise); encouragement presence (absent vs. present from stronger partner)
what_measured: exercise duration (primary outcome); self-efficacy; enjoyment; intention to exercise in future
experiment: Units performed a baseline exercise task, then were randomized to three conditions: solo, paired without encouragement, or paired with encouragement from a stronger partner. Exercise duration in the second session was compared across conditions using change scores. The primary analysis is a 2x3 ANOVA (Gender x Condition) on duration change. Secondary outcomes on psychosocial measures were also evaluated across conditions.

what_manipulated: webpage background complexity and visual simplicity (simple vs. complex backgrounds)
what_measured: attitude toward advertisement; attitude toward brand; purchase intention; attitude toward website
experiment: Units viewed web-embedded advertisements with either simple or complex background elements. Attitudes toward the ad, brand, website, and purchase intentions were measured and compared across background conditions. The study tests whether background visual complexity has a main effect on multiple attitude-related outcomes.

what_manipulated: exposure to racial diversity (student composition variation); engagement with diversity opportunities (social and curricular interactions)
what_measured: learning outcomes; educational experience quality; knowledge acquisition from cross-racial interaction
experiment: Units nested within campuses with varying racial composition were assessed on learning and experience measures. Multilevel modeling separates the effects of direct student engagement with diversity, campus racial composition, and the campus-level effect of aggregate diversity engagement by other students, holding individual engagement constant. Comparisons test whether diversity benefits operate through multiple pathways.

what_manipulated: school-level ability composition (high-ability school vs. low-ability school); moderator variables (self-regulation constructs; socioeconomic status)
experiment: Units within high-ability schools are compared to units within low-ability schools on academic self-concept. Multilevel models test whether the big-fish-little-pond effect (negative effect of school-level ability) is moderated by individual differences in self-regulation and socioeconomic status. The analysis tests generalizability of the effect across 16 student characteristics using a large international database.

what_manipulated: student sociodemographic backgrounds (racial-ethnic, socioeconomic, gender); classroom context characteristics (school socioeconomic status; achievement levels)
what_measured: teacher perceptions of child cognitive ability; accuracy of those perceptions relative to actual performance
experiment: Units (students) are nested within classrooms and schools. Teacher perceptions of literacy skills are measured and compared to objective assessments of those skills. Hierarchical linear models decompose perceptual inaccuracy into components attributable to classroom context versus teacher characteristics, controlling for actual student academic and social backgrounds. The analysis identifies whether context or teacher background better explains systematic biases.

what_manipulated: language-minority status; initial English proficiency level (fluent vs. limited); grade level progression (kindergarten through eighth grade)
what_measured: English reading growth trajectories; reading achievement growth rates across time periods
experiment: Units are followed longitudinally across elementary and middle school years. Piecewise growth modeling fits separate trajectory slopes before and after grade transitions to compare reading growth rates for native speakers, proficient language-minority learners, and limited-proficiency language-minority learners. Effects are tested with and without socioeconomic status controls to identify convergence patterns.

what_manipulated: prime type (cleanliness-related vs. neutral prime); response effort during priming (low vs. high)
what_measured: severity of moral judgment; leniency in moral ratings for transgressions
experiment: In Study 1, units complete a prime task at low effort levels and then rate moral scenarios. In Studies 2 and 2a, units are randomly assigned to either low-effort or high-effort prime task instructions before completing identical moral judgment tasks. Moral ratings are compared across prime condition and effort level. Low-effort cleanliness priming is predicted to reduce moral judgment severity; high-effort priming is predicted to show no effect.

what_manipulated: training type (intervention training with situational awareness and tactical gaze control vs. active control training resembling traditional firearms training); video-based instructional design
what_measured: decision accuracy in shoot/don't shoot scenarios; response time; time to first shot; muzzle position accuracy; tactical conduct
experiment: Units (senior police officers) are randomly assigned to intervention or control training conditions. Both groups view an educational video, then participate in practical training in a firing range. Pre- and post-test shoot/don't shoot video scenarios measure decision accuracy and response timing. A preregistered design tests whether the intervention improves decision-making and response times compared to control training.

what_manipulated: stimulus modality (unimodal visual vs. bimodal visual and auditory); congruence between flanker and target (same letter name vs. different letter mapped to same response)
what_measured: reaction time; response accuracy; flanker interference effect magnitude
experiment: In two experiments, units respond to a central target letter while task-irrelevant flanker letters are presented. In the bimodal condition, flankers are auditory and targets are visual, crossing modalities. Reaction times and accuracy are compared when flankers match the target letter name versus when they differ. The comparison tests whether cross-modal flanker effects emerge and whether they depend on stimulus identity versus response mapping.

what_manipulated: family structure (single childless vs. married vs. with children); anticipated after-work domestic activities
what_measured: work absorption; psychological immersion in work; attention focus during work hours
experiment: Study 1 compares work absorption across family structure categories in a survey of business school alumni. Study 2 uses daily diary data from university employees to test whether anticipated after-work activities mediate the relationship between family structure and work absorption. The analysis tests whether domestic responsibilities (obligatory, goal-directed) reinforce work-related mental states rather than distract from them.
